items.q: What is the difference between ozone depletion and climate change?
items.correct: Two distinct issues - ozone depletion is about UV protection (caused mainly by CFCs); climate change is about heat trapping (caused mainly by CO2).
items.random_wrong: Ozone depletion causes climate change directly.
items.why: Students conflate these as 'the atmosphere problem'. They involve different gases, different atmospheric layers, different mechanisms, and different (though sometimes connected) environmental consequences.

items.q: Why does renewable energy require energy storage solutions?
items.correct: Solar and wind generation are intermittent - storage smooths out supply and demand.
items.misconception: Renewable energy can never meet demand.
items.random_wrong: Because they generate too much energy.
items.why: Students dismiss renewables as inadequate. The challenge is intermittency, not insufficiency - the Sun sets, wind drops. Storage (batteries, pumped hydro) addresses timing, not total capacity.

items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.
Correct answer: Water. “Smoke” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many future sources were named (hydrogen, tidal, wave, fusion)?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
